data.title: Top Merger Stocks Held By Fund Managers, Mid-Q1 2025
data.description:
A narrative that I have been hearing for three months was that the new administration would change everything in terms of M&A in the United States. Softer regulatory reviews and quicker closes for deals. So far, so....not good. Two weeks ago, Juniper Networks (JNPR) had its planned merger with Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE) blocked by the Department of Justice. The companies have said that they strongly oppose the lawsuit and plan to vigorously defend the transaction in court. Time will tell what will happen in court and with future mergers under the new administration.

Each quarter, I compile the top 10 stocks held in the funds that specialize in merger stocks and share it with our Seeking Alpha community.

My rules:

  • I only count a stock if it is at least 1% of the fund's portfolio.
  • I only include stocks that are still actively trading.
  • I only choose funds where the majority of positions are merger-related.

The top stock this quarter is Kellanova (K). 25 of my 35 funds have positions in Kellanova. Half a year ago candy giant Mars Incorporated agreed to buy Kellanova for almost $36 Billion including assumed net leverage which amounts to $83.50 per share. The proposed merger would unite Mars, a global leader in pet care, snacking, and food, with Kellanova, a company known for its global snacking, international cereal and noodles. The deal is expected to close in the first half of this year. Approvals in the United States, European Commission and the United Kingdom are needed. Including Kellanova's 2.78% dividend, the deal sports approximately a 10% IRR.

In the second slot with 21 funds owning it is HashiCorp (HCP). IBM (IBM) is in the process of buying them for $35 per share. The deal has been on a second request from the Federal Trade Commission since last summer. There are only 61 cents left in the spread, but if the merger closes in the first quarter, which IBM has said it would, the IRR would be 15% or more. Fundamentally, Seeking Alpha's Quant rankings rate HashiCorp a Buy.

10) United States Steel Corporation (X) Held by 10 funds 142%

When deals get close to the expected closing date, IRR's can look crazy high. An extra month or two changes the IRR significantly.

When a fund makes an M&A stock its top holding, it signals strong conviction that the deal will ultimately close. Eight funds had Hess as its top pick.

Oversized Holdings

Some arb funds oversize the positions that they have great conviction in. As of the end of 2024, there were a good number of stocks that were at least 16% of a fund.

Conclusion

While there is no substitute for doing one's own work and developing one's unique trading/investing style, looking at what some of the top arb hedge funds are doing with their money is quite valuable. These funds have more resources, staff, and contacts than an individual investor can possibly have.

While there is no guarantee that the above deals will close, and two of them haven't already been blocked, the fact is approximately 94 percent of all mergers do close and when the specialists allocate their money to an arb position the odds even get better.

data.title: Dundee Precious Metals: Far Too Cheap To Ignore
data.description:
Gold prices continue setting new records. While mainstream media is focusing on short-term factors (such as large quantities of physical gold fleeing LME vaults for America on looming tariff fears), I believe the main drivers of the current gold bull market are far deeper. US Treasury secretary, Scott Bessent, has stated that a new monetary order is emerging, and that he wants to be part of its redesign. We don't know exactly what form the new system will take, but it is my conviction that a structural shift is in progress, and gold is going to play a central role.

An important piece of evidence is, in my opinion, the decoupling between 10-year breakeven rates and gold prices: they have both been marching higher, despite their historical anti-correlation. The reason is probably that the marginal gold buyer is no longer US gold ETFs reacting to changes in US real interest rates. Rather, the price setters are now central banks worldwide and Asian private buyers, especially from China.

Yet, despite strong gold prices, gold miners are underperforming the metal. In recent weeks, miners have done some catching up, but I believe this move is far from over.

The puzzling aspect is that the free cash flow of most miners is clearly inflecting upwards, thanks to a combination of record prices, and normalizing or even falling costs.

The result is that many gold miners are as cheap as ever, despite rising share prices. The share prices are not rising fast enough to account for the increase in profitability that has started filtering through financial results over the last few quarters.

Dundee Precious Metals (OTCPK:DPMLF)(TSX:DPM:CA) has long been a favorite of mine. It has been one of the best performing miners, up 80% since over the last 3 years. Yet, gold is up 55% over the same timeframe, and given the leveraged exposure to gold, strong operational performance, falling costs, and its world-class discovery at Coka Rakita, I would have expected Dundee to outperform gold by a more significant margin.

My conclusion is that Dundee is actually as cheap as ever.

Let's look at the math. Dundee has a market capitalization of just below $2 billion. It has no significant long-term debt. 40% of its market capitalization is in cash, providing a huge margin of safety: the company has about $800 million in the bank (about $635 million at the end of Q4 2024, plus $170 million received in January 2025 from the sale of its Tsumeb smelter in Namibia). Therefore, the EV is just $1.2 billion.

What do we get for $1.2 billion? Let's look first at some backward-looking numbers. In 2024, Dundee produced over $300 million in free cash flow. Of this, $92 million were produced in the last quarter. Gold prices are up another 10% since the beginning of the year. Considering that production in 2025 is guided to be roughly flat, all-in sustaining cost (AISC) slightly decreasing, and growth capex unchanged, Dundee could easily generate over $350 million in free cash flow in 2025. That's an almost 30% free cash flow yield.

This looks quite cheap. But perhaps the market is spooked that its assets are declining, so Dundee will be unable to maintain current production levels. Let's have a look at the company's guidance for the next three years.

Ada Tepe, a high-grade but small deposit in Bulgaria, has indeed declining production: after producing 94 Koz of gold in 2024, it is expected to produce 65-80 Koz in 2025, and finally 25-35 Koz in 2026.

Chelopech, Dundee's cornerstone asset in Bulgaria, has a more stable production profile. It produced 167 Koz in 2024, and is expected to produce 160-185 Koz in 2025, 150-165 Koz in 2026 and 155-175 Koz in 2027. Its current mineral reserve mine life extends to 2032, with a strong mineral resource base of 1.2 million ounces. The company has repeatedly managed to extend its mine life via exploration, most recently last year. With a 4,100 ha land package and several compelling exploration prospects, this trend is likely to continue.

Overall, we can expect annual average production of 200 thousand ounces over the next three years. This is a 23% decline compared with 2024. AISC are to average around $865 per ounce, in line with 2024 numbers, making Dundee one of the lowest-cost producers in the world.

Declining production is, obviously, a serious concern (even if rising gold prices over the next three years will, in my opinion, more than compensate for it). I would therefore understand market skepticism, if there were no clear plans on how to put its huge cash position to work and achieve growth. Such plans, however, do exist.

In 2023, Dundee made a big discovery at its Coka Rakita project, in Serbia. It has since put the project on high priority (given delays in advancing its Loma Larga project in Ecuador due to opposition from environmental NGOs). A feasibility study is targeted by the end of 2025. Construction could begin by mid-2026. First concentrate production is expected in 2028. Serbia is a solid jurisdiction and, given the team's operational track record, I believe these estimates are not overly optimistic.

Coka Rakita is planned to become a second cornerstone asset, similar in size to Chelopech. The PFS outlined a 10-year mine life, with annual production of about 170 thousand ounces. AISC is expected to be one of the lowest on the global cost curve, at $644 per ounce (the average AISC of the sector is probably around $1500 per ounce right now). The after-tax NPV-5% at $2500 per ounce is over $1.2 billion. In other words, the NPV of Coka Rakita alone is greater than the company's EV! Initial capex is estimated at $379 million, which can be entirely covered from cash on hand. Sustaining capex is only $29 million, which accounts for the low AISC.

There is also significant exploration upside, with already two high-grade copper-gold discoveries (Dumitru Potok and Frasen prospects), which are not accounted for in the Coka Rakita PFS.

Besides Coka Rakita, Dundee also has a potential growth pipeline in Ecuador.

At its Tierras Coloradas concession, the company reported some solid intersects in 2023, following a 2,700-metre diamond drilling program. A 10,000-metre drilling program commenced in August 2023. This is a very early-stage exploration asset, but it provides further free optionality.

The Loma Larga project, also in Ecuador, could be a true game changer, if it ever gets into production. The asset could sustain annual production of 200 thousand ounces over its first 5 years, and is currently undergoing permitting. The company has encountered significant opposition during the process due to environmental concerns. For a while, it seemed that Loma Larga was going to become a stranded asset. However, the company has quietly achieved some recent successes. First of all, there is now an investment protection agreement in place with the government of Ecuador, which has always been strongly supportive of the project. Dundee has recently completed the environmental consultations, with the local communities voting overall in favor. Baseline ecosystem and water studies were submitted in Q3, 2024. Free, prior and informed consultations are set to start in 2025, a crucial stepping stone to unlock the project. Undoubtedly, the path is still long, but since the market is valuing Loma Larga at zero, this is an additional option card that one gets essentially for free.

In conclusion, I see Dundee as a profoundly mispriced company. Concerns about declining production are overblown, taking into account the pipeline of projects available in its portfolio. The next three years are going to be a bridging period. Production will be decreasing to stagnant, but this phase is necessary to unlock the next phase, with the development of Coka Rakita, which will double production from 2028 and lower costs. In any case, the current valuation is totally disconnected from the cash flow generation Dundee is already capable of. The company is trading at an EV / FCF multiple of just 4x, and it is targeting returning $200 million to shareholders via buybacks (a more than 10% yield). This is far too cheap to ignore.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

The gross performance results presented do not reflect the deduction of investment advisory fees. Returns will be reduced by such advisory fees and other expenses as described in the individual contract and, where applicable, Form ADV Part 2A.

Net returns are calculated by subtracting the highest applicable Managed Account fee from the pure gross or gross composite return. The Managed Account fee includes all charges for trading costs, portfolio management, custody and other administrative fees. Actual fees may vary dep ending on, among other things, the applicable fee schedule and portfolio size. The fees are available on request and may be found in Form ADV Part 2A.

Investment environment

  • The U.S. short-term fixed income market posted a slightly negative return for the quarter, with the Bloomberg 1-3 Year US Government/Credit Index returning -0.02%. Credit-spread sectors outperformed U.S. Treasuries.
  • The U.S. economy remained resilient, as third-quarter 2024 gross domestic product (GDP) growth came in at 2.8% and the unemployment rate held steady around 4.1%. While Treasury markets sold off, risk markets reacted favorably to the decisive Republican victory in the November election, due to expectations for expansionary fiscal policies and deregulation.
  • After starting the rate cutting cycle with a 50-basis-point (BPs) cut in September, the Federal Reserve (Fed) took a decidedly more hawkish tone at its December meeting, with the revised Fed dot plot implying just 50 bps of rate cuts in 2025. The yield on the 10-year U.S. Treasury ended the quarter at 4.57%, relative to 3.78% at the end of September.
  • Investors weighed the expected positive economic implications of the incoming administration with the slower anticipated pace of rate cuts. Corporate investment-grade credit spreads tightened 9 bps to 80 bps, while high-yield spreads tightened 8 bps to 287 bps.

Portfolio review

Outperformance during the quarter was driven by our overweight allocation to spread risk. Spread sectors broadly outperformed on the back of relatively healthy economic data and positive investor sentiment following the outcome of the November election.

The key contributors included overweight allocations to securitized credit sectors, such as collateralized loan obligations (CLOs), non-agency mortgages, commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS), and asset-backed securities (ABS). An allocation to agency MBS detracted. While overall asset allocation was beneficial, overall security selection weighed on relative performance.

Within our interest-rate risk exposure, we entered the quarter with a small duration underweight, which contributed as Treasury yields rose. We added duration as yields rose and ended the quarter with a neutral duration position.

The portfolio remains well diversified with exposure to an array of fixed income sectors, while still limiting our exposure to below-investment-grade investments. This portfolio structure seeks to capture the benefits of diversification and attractive relative value opportunities while limiting downside risk.

Manager outlook

As we enter 2025, we believe investors should seek to take advantage of three positive drivers impacting fixed income markets: the strong U.S. economy, the Fed having initiated its rate-cutting cycle, and the likely normalization of rate-spread correlations due to lower inflation.

Regarding the U.S. economy, corporates and consumers remain largely resilient. While corporate spreads are tight, we believe that strong fundamentals – including the favorable macroeconomic environment and reasonable leverage levels – will continue to support these valuations. Additionally, securitized sectors have continued to trade cheaply relative to corporates, offering attractive yields and the potential for spread tightening.

Furthermore, now that the Fed has begun its rate-cutting cycle and rates markets have priced in a slower pace of cuts, we believe bond investors may lean into the yields on offer and be less concerned with fighting rising rates. Even if inflation proves to be stickier due to the incoming administration’s economic policies, we think much of this risk is already priced in.

Real-time data indicates that inflation has continued to moderate toward the central bank’s target of 2%, and we believe that a resurgence of inflation above 3% is unlikely. Rates and spreads historically have been negatively correlated when inflation is below 3% – resulting in reduced portfolio volatility, as any material widening in credit spreads may be somewhat counterbalanced by falling rates.

Overall, we favor an overweight to credit spread risk amid the strong fundamental and technical backdrop, and an overweight to duration risk due to expectations for further rate cuts and a re-steepening of the yield curve. The market is currently pricing in a terminal rate around 4%, which is notably higher than the Fed’s estimate of 3.1%. We think the actual terminal rate will settle at a level below 4%, but we also expect the Fed’s estimate to move higher.

We believe it is important for investors to maximize income per unit of risk and limit drawdown risks in the present environment. In our view, the current market is more of a “carry” and bond pickers market, where investors are rewarded by clipping relatively high coupons and picking individual bonds with positively skewed risk-return expectations, as opposed to making macro bets.

Moreover, we think allocating to sectors that are trading at cheaper relative valuations – such as loans over high yield, or collateralized loan obligations and ABS over corporates – will be key in 2025. There likely will be continued noise and volatility around the new administration’s policies on deregulation, taxes, tariffs, and immigration, which we believe may create alpha opportunities for nimble, active investment managers.

Definitions

Basis point (BP) equals 1/100 of a percentage point. 1 bp = 0.01%, 100 bps = 1%.

Duration measures a bond price’s sensitivity to changes in interest rates. The longer a bond’s duration, the higher its sensitivity to changes in interest rates and vice versa. A yield curve plots the yields (interest rate) of bonds with equal credit quality but differing maturity dates. Typically bonds with longer maturities have higher yields.

Credit spread is the difference in yield between securities with similar maturity but different credit quality. Widening spreads generally indicate deteriorating creditworthiness of corporate borrowers, and narrowing indicate improving.

Securitized products, such as mortgage- and asset-backed securities, are more sensitive to interest rate changes, have extension and prepayment risk, and are subject to more credit, valuation, and liquidity risk than other fixed income securities.

10-year Treasury yield is the interest rate on U.S. Treasury bonds that will mature 10 years from the date of purchase.

Quantitative tightening (QT) is a government monetary policy occasionally used to decrease the money supply by either selling government securities or letting them mature and removing them from its cash balances.

Monetary Policy refers to the policies of a central bank, aimed at influencing the level of inflation and growth in an economy. It includes controlling interest rates and the supply of money. Diversification neither assures a profit nor eliminates the risk of experiencing investment losses.

Correlation measures the degree to which two variables move in relation to each other. A value of 1.0 implies movement in parallel, -1.0 implies movement in opposite directions, and 0.0 implies no relationship.

Volatility measures risk using the dispersion of returns for a given investment.

Alpha compares risk-adjusted performance relative to an index. Positive alpha means outperformance on a risk-adjusted basis.

data.title: The Monarch Cement Company: Despite A Downgrade, The Firm Still Justifies Upside
data.description:
One company that I really like and have a pretty good track record with is The Monarch Cement Company (OTCPK:MCEM). Since I last reaffirmed the company as a ‘strong buy’ candidate back in August of 2024, shares are up 15.8% while the S&P 500 is up 13.7%. And since I originally rated the company a ‘buy’ Back in December of 2023, the stock is up 50.7%. By comparison, the broader market has achieved upside of only 28.2%. Despite this monumental move higher, shares of the business still seem slightly attractive. Revenue, profits, and cash flows are all growing nicely, and the firm has a rather robust balance sheet. To be honest, where the stock is today would normally justify a downgrade to a ‘hold’ rating. However, because of the aforementioned balance sheet strength, I believe the stock deserves a little bit of wiggle room.

Growth continues

For those not familiar with The Monarch Cement Company, the company serves as a producer and seller of Portland cement. This is a type of cement that is used to make ready mixed concrete that is used in things like highways, bridges, buildings, and more. This might not seem like the most exciting space to be in. But the fact of the matter is that this is an industry that is almost certain to continue growing for the foreseeable future. For this year, for instance, total construction starts in the US are estimated to be about 8.5% higher than they were the previous year. This should be driven by a 12% rise on the residential side as interest rates fall. However, even the non-residential side is expected to grow by 6.9%. Overall non-residential building growth is expected to be even higher than total nonresidential growth at 8%. Meanwhile, experts believe that civil engineering growth should be around 5.3%.

It is worth mentioning that not every source agrees that growth will be this high for the year as a whole. On the non-residential side, for instance, the American Institute of Architects believes that non-residential construction will only grow by 2.2% this year and 2.6% next year, with some of that weakness driven by the threat of tariffs on other nations and retaliatory tariffs. In late November of last year, however, another source, pegged the growth for the industry as a whole at about 8.6% for 2025. While it also estimated that the greatest of this growth would be centered around the residential side of things, there were estimates that called for strong demand for things like bridges and highways. On the bridges side of things, growth is expected to be about 11% year over year. And for highways, it's expected to be 14.2%.

Regardless, the industry as a whole is almost certain to expand by at least something this year and will probably expand a bit next year as well unless the economy takes a big turn to the south. Already, fundamental data associated with The Monarch Cement Company is positive, so I imagine that trend will continue moving forward as well. Take the most recent quarter for which data is available, which would be the third quarter of the 2024 fiscal year. Overall sales for that time came in at $80 million. That translated to a 10% increase over the $72.7 million reported the previous year.

As a small company that's not listed on a major exchange, The Monarch Cement Company doesn't really provide much in the way of detail. All they revealed was that, year over year, revenue growth was fairly even across both of its core operations. Its cement business, for instance, saw sales climb by 8.9%, while it's ready mixed concrete business grew by 12.1%.

On the bottom line, we saw growth as well. Net profits nearly doubled from $15 million to $26.4 million. However, this seems to have been more related to the fact that, during the most recent quarter, the company booked a $5.3 million unrealized gain on its equity investments. That compares to the $4.5 million loss reported the same time the previous year. Other profitability metrics also grew, but not by as much. Operating cash flow expanded from $21.5 million to $29.3 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $19.2 million to $27.9 million. Lastly, EBITDA for the company increased from $26.3 million to $30.9 million.

For the first nine months of 2024, The Monarch Cement Company achieved growth as well. Revenue of $202.5 million beat out the $194.6 million the company reported the previous year. Most of this growth came from its ready mixed concrete business. During this window of time, its revenue jumped 7.3% from $80.5 million to $86.4 million. However, its cement business also saw growth of 3.1% from $131.9 million to $136.1 million. As was the case with the third quarter on its own, the firm also saw an expansion on its bottom line. Net income grew from $50.4 million to $54.5 million.

This was in spite of the fact that its unrealized gains on equity investments actually dropped from $7.9 million to $5.5 million. Realized gains, however, increased from $0.8 million to $1.5 million, while dividend income expanded from $1.2 million to $1.5 million. There were other contributors as well, but you get the idea. As net profits grew, other profitability metrics increased also. Operating cash flow expanded from $37.4 million to $46.6 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $52.4 million to $60.3 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $62.2 million to $68.6 million.

In the absence of estimates provided by management, the best thing that we can do in terms of valuing the company is to annualize the results that we have seen so far for the year, doing this, we get net profits of $79.7 million, adjusted operating cash flow of $83.9 million, and EBITDA of $95.5 million. Using these estimates, and the historical results for the 2023 fiscal year, we can see in the chart above how the stock is currently priced. This would normally put the company teetering between being fairly valued and slightly undervalued. However, there are some other things going for it.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
The Monarch Cement Company 10.7 10.2 8.3
Knife River Corporation (KNF) 33.0 17.4 13.7
Eagle Materials (EXP) 18.6 16.1 11.9
Vulcan Materials (VMC) 43.7 25.5 20.3
Martin Marietta Materials (MLM) 17.0 25.4 11.3
James Hardie Industries (JHX) 33.6 18.1 16.8

For starters, the stock is attractively priced compared to similar firms. In the table above, I compared it with five such businesses. Using each of the three valuation scenarios, I found that it ended up being cheaper than any of these businesses. Another thing to take into consideration here is the price to book multiple. In the chart below, you can see that, compared to how the stock was valued in the past, it does look rather pricey today. The subsequent chart stacks the company up against the same five businesses. And in this case, we can see that only one of the five firms is cheaper than it is.

In addition to being cheap, the company also has what I would consider to be a fortress balance sheet. At the end of the most recent quarter, the firm had no debt on its books. It also enjoyed $59 million in cash and cash equivalents. But that's not all. It also had Investments in affiliate companies of $16.6 million, on top of another $62.4 million in long-term investments. On top of giving the company tremendous financial flexibility, this also helps to explain the big swings in profitability caused by unrealized investment gains and the strong investment gains the company has also achieved. This certainly deserves something of a premium.

Takeaway

Based on the data provided, I must say that I remain bullish about The Monarch Cement Company. The stock isn't as cheap as it was previously. However, I believe that its continued growth, its relative valuation, and its strong balance sheet, all justify a ‘buy’ rating, which translates to a downgrade from the 'strong buy' I have had the company at for a bit.

data.title: DraftKings: Incredible Customer Acquisition, Terrible Price (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
For the most part, this earnings season, big momentum names have continued to extend their recent wins. DraftKings (NASDAQ:DKNG), the most recognizable brand in sports betting, is one of the beneficiaries of this trend: the company achieved a significant "beat and raise" in the fourth quarter that delivered a swift ~15% boost to its share price.

DraftKings sits on top of many tailwinds: additional states legalizing sports gambling, a growing casino/iGaming business, and economies of scale that are driving better hold percentages on its sportsbook. But we have to ask ourselves: after such a fierce rise over the past year, does DraftKings really have more room to shoot higher?

I last wrote a bearish note on DraftKings in November, when the stock was trading closer to $40 per share. I acknowledge that my pessimism on DraftKings was short-sighted, and I didn't anticipate the outsized performance that DraftKings delivered in Q4, particular with regard to new customer acquisition and more favorable sportsbook hold percentages (i.e., the margin that the house, in this case DraftKings, earns from its customers' total wagers). With this in mind, I'm upgrading DraftKings to a neutral rating.

I now see a relatively more balanced bull-and-bear case for DraftKings at current share prices. On the bright side for this company:

  • DraftKings still has a broad population in the U.S. that is still pending legalization. Currently, DraftKings only operates in states that represent 49% of the U.S. population (major holdouts include the state of California). As more states look to legalize sports gambling and add to their tax revenue, DraftKings could stand to benefit.
  • Growing appeal of one-time events. The company noted record wagers on the Tyson/Paul boxing match in November. Netflix (NFLX) has signaled its interest in producing more of these live events, and many of its streaming competitors could follow suit. A greater number of these highly publicized specials could drive even more activity to DraftKings' betting platform.

At the same time, however, I'm wary of the following risks:

  • Volatile trends. In two out of four quarters in FY24, the company blamed poor profit performance (and lowered its annual adjusted EBITDA guidance) due to unfavorable sports outcomes. These are unpredictable and highly likely to recur.
  • Can the company hit its guidance? DraftKings expects to quintuple its adjusted EBITDA in FY25, which is a very lofty goal, even considering the company's ~30%+ revenue growth expectations (more in the next section).

All in all, especially after the recent run-up, I don't see much room for DraftKings to run higher. Continue to watch this stock, but don't rush to buy.

Q4 download

Let's now go through DraftKings' latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

DraftKings' revenue grew 13% y/y to $1.39 billion, in-line with Wall Street's expectations. We note that revenue growth did decelerate sharply versus 39% y/y in Q3.

Let's start with the good news first: the company managed a tremendous amount of new customer sign-ups, which sets a strong base for future growth. As shown in the chart below, the company added ~800k new customers in the quarter to end with 10.1 million on the platform, which was much higher than the 500k customers it signed up in the preceding Q4.

Owing to its larger sportsbook and betting mix, the company also improved its structural hold percentage (which is the company's margin on its customers' bets), with Q4 climbing to a record 10.5% - 70bps higher y/y. It expects further improvement to ~11.0% in FY25.

Offering a hint at how performance is trending in FY25, the company notes that the first few months of Q1, buoyed by the Super Bowl, also handled record volume of bets while also demonstrating strong customer retention. Per CEO Jason Robbin's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

In January, our core value drivers resulted in revenue and adjusted EBITDA that exceeded our expectations for the month with an actual Sportsbook hold percentage of 11%. Additionally, month-to-date through February 11, our acquisition, retention and engagement continue to be strong, and our actual Sportsbook hold percentage was 13%. The Super Bowl, one of our tentpole acquisition and engagement days, was a successful event for the company. On Super Bowl Sunday, our customer acquisition was a bright spot as DraftKings Sportsbook app reached #1 in the App Store in the sports category and #3 across all apps."

Here, however, is where I continue to be quite nervous: can DraftKings really hit its profitability targets for FY25? After all, the company has a track record for falling short of its guidance, having lowered its expectations in FY24 due to unfavorable sports outcomes. For FY25, it's expecting $0.9-$1.0 billion of adjusted EBITDA, with the midpoint representing 5.2x growth over FY24 adjusted EBITDA of FY24.

Three pieces behind this guidance make me skeptical.

  • The first is that the company has added back a massive $333 million of unfavorable sports outcomes in FY24 to get to its target (we note that this addback is larger than $229 million when it first issued the $0.9-$1.0 billion target in Q3, indicating that "unfavorable sports outcomes" have gotten worse in Q4 despite the company's improved hold and its bullish execution). The company is treating FY24's relative unprofitability due to long-shot parlays hitting more frequently, but we have to consider: is this just a part of the sportsbook business? We have virtually no guarantee that long-shot bets in customers' favor will not recur in FY25.
  • The second potentially unrealistic piece is the revenue growth expectation. The company is expecting 34% y/y growth in revenue, which is a sharp acceleration from 13% growth in Q4. Now, we do note that DraftKings is starting FY25 with 10.1 million customers, up 42% from where it started FY24. But at the same time, as of the start of FY25 it's live in states representing 49% of the population, with barely any new legalizations in FY24 (it had started FY24 at 46% of the U.S. population). If customer retention and gambling activity remains high (as the company indicated is true through the month of February), this guidance is plausible, but seemingly a stretch goal.
  • The third concern: the incremental $1.2 billion in y/y revenue growth (the portion that wasn't already added back for unfavorable sports outcomes) is expected to generate $436 million of adjusted EBITDA, or a 36% adjusted EBITDA contribution margin. Considering DraftKings' adjusted EBITDA margin for FY24 was just 4%, this also seems quite a big stretch. Yes, DraftKings is improving its structural profitability alongside economies of scale: but a ~30-point leap in margins on next year's incremental revenue seems too good to be true.

Valuation and key takeaways

What keeps me firmly sidelined on DraftKings' stock, however, is the fact that even if DraftKings manages to hit its lofty targets, the company already trades at an immense valuation against it.

At current share prices near $53, DraftKings trades at a market cap of $26.08 billion. After we net off the $788.3 million of cash and $1.26 billion of convertible debt on DraftKings' latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $26.55 billion.

Against the $950 million midpoint of the company's FY25 adjusted EBITDA target range, DraftKings already trades at 28.0x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA. The stock is priced for perfection - even though FY24 has shown us that the company's business trends are far from predictable and smooth.

DraftKings deserves merit for its sharp customer expansion and its improving sportsbook hold: but in this case, I believe the company's guidance targets (and the stock market) are getting ahead of reality. I'd prefer to remain on the sidelines for now and invest elsewhere.

data.title: Unilever: Growth Disappoints But Business Efficiency Process Continues
data.description:
FY 2024 was not particularly appreciated by the market; in fact, the stock plummeted 9% in the days following publication. As we shall see, revenue growth compared to FY 2023 was modest, but there was an improvement in profitability (Gross Margin 45%, +280 bps vs. FY 2023). The most worrying aspect relates to an expected subdued first half of 2025, mainly due to price increases in palm oil, surfactants, and cocoa. Commodity inflation will weigh €800 million on COGS, and the company expects a pricing adjustment starting in mid-2025 to keep profitability intact. However, since volumes have not grown much this year, analysts wonder whether higher prices might not lead to negative volumes.

Underlying sales growth in 2025 is expected to increase between 3-5% and the operating margin should see a modest improvement, especially in H2 2024. Meanwhile, discussions continue for the Ice Cream segment division.

Highlights Q4 2024/FY 2024

Before starting to comment on the results, for those who do not know, Unilever (NYSE:UL) (OTCPK:UNLYF) considers underlying sales and not sales in its reports. The reason is that underlying sales exclude the impact of acquisitions, divestitures, and currency fluctuations; therefore, they facilitate a better comparison with previous financial statements. That said, let's begin.

Underlying sales growth increased 4.20% over FY 2023, thanks to +2.90% in volumes and +1.30% in pricing. Anyway, Power Brands, i.e., those that Unilever focuses on the most and generate >75% of sales, saw higher growth, +5.30%. Again, pricing was low, +1.40%, but volume growth was higher, +3.80%. The fact that Power Brands are growing faster is quite encouraging, as in recent years the company has decided to focus primarily on them, and in some cases sell brands with lower return on capital.

Unilever is in a phase of business efficiency, and is focusing its capital on the brands with the highest demand and profit margins. Here, economies of scale and cost management take center stage; in fact, the company expects from its productivity program to save about €800 million in 2025. Part of the savings will go to finance the separation of the Ice Cream segment.

The Ice Cream segment is not doing so bad, in fact, it achieved 3.70% growth in FY 2024. The problem is that it is no longer part of Unilever's vision, now focused on the Personal Care, Beauty & Wellbeing segment, which, by the way, has achieved the most growth this year. After all, if Unilever's goal is to improve business efficiency and eliminate duplicate costs, it is difficult to succeed if you want to compete in two totally different markets. In other words, to try to excel in two segments, you risked not growing in either one.

By the end of 2025, Ice Cream will be demerged from Unilever, and listed on the New York, Amsterdam, and London stock exchanges: on its own, it can exploit its maximum potential.

Beauty & Wellbeing is the segment that grew the most and recorded a 6.50% improvement in underlying sales. Much of the growth came from increased volumes, +5.10%, and only a small part from pricing, +1.30%. These results make me think that Unilever is gaining market share by keeping pricing low, but the underlying operating margin improved anyway: 19.40%, +70 vs. FY2023.

Sunsilk, its major hair care brand, grew at high-single digits; Vaseline and Dave & Pond's, brands in the Skin Care category, posted double-digit growth. In Wellbeing, Liquid IV & Nutrafol also grew at double digits.

Personal Care is another segment the company relies heavily on and was the second in terms of growth: +5.20% vs. FY2023. In addition, the underlying operating margin is the highest among the segments: 22.10%, +190 bps vs. FY2023.

Much of this positive performance comes from strong double-digit growth in Dove, Rexona & Axe deodorants. To date, Dove is responsible for 40% of Personal Care segment sales.

Home Care grew little, only 2.90%, but gained market share given volumes up 4%. Pricing was negative because commodity deflation was greater in the powder format than in the liquid format, resulting in negative pricing in several emerging markets. The underlying operating margin for this sector improved by 220 bps, but it remains quite low (14.50%) compared to previous ones.

Foods had a mixed performance. Sales rose little (2.60% vs. FY 2023) due to volume growth virtually absent for several quarters. Still, the underlying operating margin increased 270 bps to 21.30%. This segment is not to be underestimated given its profitability, but it is clearly experiencing headwinds in terms of growth. There are mainly two causes:

  • The target market is not expanding.
  • Unilever has too many unprofitable brands that it needs to sell.

Knorr and Hellmann's have performed well considering the difficulties in the industry in which they operate and account for 60% of Foods' sales, but the remaining 40% is disappointing expectations.

Foods delivered growth of 2.6% in 2024, with 2.4% price and volume at 0.2%, amidst an overall deceleration in the market. In the fourth quarter, Foods grew 2.6% with 2.1% from price and 0.5% from volume. Our two largest brands, Knorr and Hellmann’s, which account for around 60% of Foods turnover, outperformed the Foods average. Knorr enhanced its global leadership in the bouillon and seasonings category. Hellmann’s achieved volume-led growth through the continued success of its flavored mayo ranges and premium formats.

CEO Fernando Fernandez Q4 2024 conference call.

As already anticipated, Unilever is going through a phase where it wants to focus only on the few certainties, in fact, it has declared that the brands in the Foods segment will be downsized. During 2025, the sale of Unox, Conimex, and Zwan will be made official.

In terms of geography, Unilever is struggling mainly in Asia Pacific Africa, which is one of the most important areas for the company's long-term growth. Price increases have been moderate, but volume growth has been only 1.80%. This situation is likely to last for another few months, but from mid-2025 improvements are expected in both China and Indonesia.

In China, we are strengthening our business during a market slowdown. We are accelerating our portfolio premiumization to drive growth in the premium and super-premium segments through innovations behind unmissably superior Power Brands. We are serving emerging channels better through social-first demand creation models and direct-to-consumer models. We are transforming our route-to-market, to effectively address lower tier cities and smaller format stores.

In Indonesia, we have long-standing portfolio and brand proposition issues, which will take several quarters to fix. In the short term, we are correcting misaligned investments and price structures across channels and resetting stock levels in retail. We are expanding our direct and indirect coverage with fewer and bigger distributors.

CEO Fernando Fernandez Q4 2024 conference call.

All in all, it seems that management has these issues under control and is taking action to solve them.

Conclusion

Overall, I expected something more from this quarterly and also about FY 2025 guidance, but I think the situation remains stable. Unilever's sales growth is unlikely to exceed 5%, but we have to consider that it is in a transition phase. Past, Unilever made too many acquisitions that turned out to be disappointing, and today it wants to streamline its brand portfolio. After all, Power Brands generate 75% of revenue, and the remaining 25% is made up of brands with fluctuating performance.

Inevitably, this choice will lead to reduced revenues, but profit margins will benefit. It is much better to rely on a few successful brands than on many with mixed performance. The focus should be on what is demanded by the market, and I agree with the choice to split the Ice Cream segment.

Unilever can be described as a kind of European Procter & Gamble, but its profit margin is half. The business model is very similar, yet PG consistently achieves far better profitability: this is where Unilever needs to work. Management seems to have figured out where the problem is, but it will not be easy to fill the gap in the coming years.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Stability-Instability Paradox
data.description:
Market Shakes Off Inflation Data

I am back from traveling, and we have a good bit to catch up on since our last report. If you missed it, I provided an update on Tuesday, updating all the weekly technical and statistical data we produce. Most noteworthy in that report was the sharp increase in money flows into the market despite the tariff announcement by the Trump administration and the latest inflation reports.

On Thursday, the market broke out of the bullish consolidation over the last few weeks, successfully retesting and holding support at the 50-DMA. Notably, the bullish trend remains intact, and retail investors continue to pour money into the market, with money flows reaching typical peak levels. With the market elevated, downside risk over the next few weeks will likely be contained to recent January lows. What would cause such a correction is unknown, but if money flows begin to reverse, such will likely provide the evidence needed to rebalance risks accordingly.

The bullish bias is evident, as witnessed by the recent surge in retail money flows into leveraged ETFs and speculative options trading. However, as is always the case, whenever investors are crowded on “one side of the boat,” it is often a decent contrarian signal to be a bit more cautious. Furthermore, while there is currently no evidence of a catalyst for a correction, it is worth noting that we are entering into the seasonally weak part of February.

While this is the average of daily market returns, it does not guarantee that market weakness will present itself. But it is worth being aware of the potential possibility of such a development.

Speaking of excess, Sentiment Trader recently did a great piece on the market’s Sharpe ratio. The conclusion of their report is worth considering.

“When the going gets easy for investors, it’s natural to let one’s guard down and become complacent. That’s a dangerous condition for all but the longest-term, long-term, unleveraged investors. Markets can be their most dangerous when they look the safest.

Using the Sharpe ratio as a proxy for how good it’s been for U.S. investors, we see above that there aren’t many times in history when it’s been better than the past six months, and there are signs that it’s ending. That can mean more volatility, but it doesn’t necessarily mean negative returns. The biggest takeaway has been moderate returns, with much more of a two-way market than investors had gotten used to in the months prior.”

An extended period of speculative complacency in the markets has markedly increased the Sharpe ratio. The problem is that long periods of complacency, a function of price stability, are often followed by periods of instability.

Such is the core of our discussion this week.

Stability Leads To Complacency

“Only those that risk going too far can possibly find out how far one can go.” – T.S. Eliot

As discussed on Tuesday, retail investors are currently “all-in” in the market. Such is not surprising given the long period of stability in the markets with continually rising prices.

“The market defies more negative news because retail investors continue to step in and “buy the dip.” In our recent Bull Bear reports, we discussed the push by retail investors, but looking at retail sentiment is quite remarkable. Since the pandemic, retail investors have never been this bullish on the stock market. Such is amazing, given that their mailboxes are not being stuffed with government stimulus checks”

“At the same time, their optimism about stock market returns is supported by putting their money where their mouth is.”

These periods of stability have always led to high levels of investor complacency concerning risk. However, historically speaking, such periods of complacency are often built on rationalizations with weak underpinnings. Investors are confident that the Fed will continue cutting interest rates, easing monetary policy, and supporting higher future stock valuations. However, that expectation may be misguided as the Fed remains unconcerned about any near-term recessionary impact. However, policy actions by the current Administration to reduce the deficit, cut Government employment, and impose tariffs are factors that could slow economic growth rates more than anticipated. Such is particularly the case now as evidence of weakening employment and consumers is emerging.

As Michael Lebowitz commented recently:

” While labor market data is generally good, there are signs the labor market is at a standstill. Continuing jobless claims are steadily rising at their highest level in over three years. The JOLTS hires rate is at ten-year lows. While the number of layoffs remains low, employers aren’t hiring either. Accordingly, the broad labor market data may seem good, but the chart below and other data should give the Fed pause so that consumers may start to spend less and save more. As if the chart below wasn’t concerning. It shows employment expectations are also plummeting. Similar changes in expectations have led to a higher unemployment rate previously.”

Furthermore, expectations of real household incomes do not suggest a robust consumer backdrop.

The rise in part-time employment, slowing hiring rates, and increased continuing jobless claims indicate a weaker labor market. Historically, overestimating employment strength has led the Fed to delay necessary rate cuts. Once economic conditions deteriorate further, the Fed is forced to reverse course.

Unfortunately, the Fed is often “behind the curve” in anticipating such risk, leading to more aggressive monetary policy actions. In other words, market stability leads to policy complacency, which eventually evolves into instability.

The Stability-Instability Paradox

This is the problem facing the Fed.

Investors have been led to believe that no matter what happens, the Fed can bail out the markets and keep the bull market going for a while longer. Or rather, as Dr. Irving Fisher once uttered:

“Stocks have reached a permanently high plateau.”

Interestingly, the Fed depends on market participants and consumers believing this idea. With the entirety of the financial ecosystem now more heavily levered than ever due to the Fed’s profligate measures of suppressing interest rates and flooding the system with excessive liquidity over the last 15 years, the “instability of stability paradox” is now the most significant risk.

“The ‘stability/instability paradox’ assumes that all players are rational and such rationality implies an avoidance of complete destruction. In other words, all players will act rationally, and no one will push ‘the big red button.‘”

The Fed is highly dependent on this assumption as it provides the “room” needed to navigate the risks that have built up in the system. The risks of something breaking have increased substantially from elevated market valuations to exceptionally low credit spreads. As we saw in March 2023, the rise in interest rates nearly took down the regional banking sector until the Federal Reserve was forced to step in with the “Bank Term Funding Program.” Fortunately, that banking risk did not become a financial contagion, and the Federal Reserve maintained stability across the markets.

However, the key to that stability depends on “everyone acting rationally.”

Unfortunately, maintaining permanent stability has never been achieved over the long term.

The Fed’s Problem – Being Late

The most serious risk facing the Fed is individuals’ behavioral biases. Throughout history, the market has been plagued with unexpected, exogenous risks that fell outside the Federal Reserve’s regulatory abilities. Despite the best of intentions, changes to monetary policies, combined with investor complacency, preceded mild to disastrous outcomes.

  • In the early 70s, it was the “Nifty Fifty” stocks,
  • Then, Mexican and Argentine bonds a few years after that
  • “Portfolio Insurance” was the “thing” in the mid-80s
  • Fed rates led to the bond market crash in 1994.
  • Dot.com anything was an excellent investment in 1999
  • Real estate has been a boom/bust cycle roughly every other decade, but 2008 was a doozy
  • Today, it’s leveraged ETFs, higher risk credit, and “Artificial Intelligence” everything.

The risk to this entire house of cards is a credit-related event. As Michael Lebowitz noted recently:

“Despite the tight corporate spreads, the difference between the S&P 500 earnings yield and corporate bonds is negative 2%. The Bloomberg graph on the right shows that the spread hasn’t been that tight since 2008. Stocks are riskier, yet corporate earnings yield less than corporate bonds. The graph further confirms very high equity valuations, suggesting investors’ earnings growth expectations are much loftier than historical earnings growth rates.”

“People are skewing toward assets that are giving you more and more upside. You’re really just trying to see people hit home runs here more and more.” – Bloomberg

What happens if, or should I say when, passive funds become large net sellers of credit risk? In that event, those indiscriminate sellers will have to find highly discriminating buyers who–you guessed it–will be asking lots of questions. Liquidity for the passive universe–and thus the credit markets generally–may become problematic. Furthermore, the significant decline in market liquidity indeed suggests rising risks.

If there is a liquidity issue, the risk to “uninformed investors” is substantially higher than most realize.

Risk concentration always seems rational initially, and those early successes create a self-reinforcing behavioral sentiment.

As noted, stability is an illusion of everyone acting rationally. Unfortunately, when it all goes “pear-shaped,” rational calm quickly turns into irrational panic.

Investors Are Ignoring The Cracks In Stability

Stability is acceptable until something occurs that causes instability. Since October 2022, the market has steadily risen despite higher interest rates, inflation, and slowing economic growth. Changes to the Fed’s outlook, or as recently as tariffs and DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK), have caused market pullbacks. However, market stability has primarily been contained to a relatively narrow range of +/- 1% in daily price movements.

The chart below shows the importance of paying attention to volatility. As is always the case, periods of “low volatility” beget “high volatility.” For example, following the 2020 “Pandemic shutdown,” a period noted by increased ranges of daily price movements (high volatility), investors experienced an 18-month winning streak with low volatility. That period ended with the Russian invasion of Ukraine and the Federal Reserve embarking upon one of its most aggressive rate hiking campaigns since the late 70s.

However, alternating periods of low to high volatility and vice versa have been a hallmark of the financial markets since the turn of the century. What should be obvious is that these periods of low volatility are truncated by unexpected, exogenous events that cause market participants to reevaluate consensus expectations. For example, in 2000, the collapse of Enron called into question the entirety of the “Dot.com” thesis. 2008 Lehman’s failure ended the belief that “subprime was contained.” Today, the market is highly confident in superior economic growth and sustained and elevated levels of earnings growth due to “Artificial Intelligence.” What disrupts that thesis is unknown but is the most significant risk to investors today.

It is also worth noting that periods of stability have historically been truncated by the Federal Reserve and its rate-cutting cycle.

The reason, of course, is that by the time the Federal Reserve is cutting rates aggressively, something has broken in the financial system. While that has not happened yet, it does not mean it won’t.

The Single Biggest Risk To Your Money

In extremely long bull market cycles, investors become “willfully blind” to the underlying inherent risks. Or rather, it is the “hubris” of investors that they are now “smarter than the market.”

Yet, the list of concerns remains despite being completely ignored by investors and the mainstream media.

  • Growing economic ambiguities in the U.S. and abroad.
  • Political instability.
  • The failure of fiscal policy to ‘trickle down.’
  • A pivot towards easing in global monetary policy (global economic weakness).
  • Geopolitical risks from Trade Wars to Iran.
  • Un-inversions of yield curves.
  • Potential deteriorating in earnings and corporate profit margins.
  • Record levels of private and public debt.

None of that matters for now, as the markets hope for continued easing in monetary accommodation. The more the market rises, the more reinforced the belief that “this time is different” becomes.

Yes, our investment portfolios remain invested on the long side for now. (Although we continue to carry slightly higher levels of cash and hedges.)

However, that will change rapidly at the first sign of the “instability of stability.”

How We Are Trading It

Given the market uncertainty, the high levels of complacency, and the risks to stability, managing portfolio risks is worth considering. That is why we have started rebalancing portfolio risk accordingly. With both technical and sentiment readings suggesting the short-term market risks are elevated, it is wise to take some “small” actions now, which you will likely appreciate later.

  1. Tighten up stop-loss levels to current support levels for each position.
  2. Hedge portfolios against more significant market declines.
  3. Take profits in positions that have been big winners.
  4. Sell laggards and losers.
  5. Raise cash and rebalance portfolios to target weightings.

Therefore, from a portfolio management perspective, we have to trade the market we have rather than the one we think should be. This can make battling emotions difficult from week to week. However, as noted, we expect a correction sooner rather than later, providing a better risk/reward opportunity to increase equity exposure if needed.

SimpleVisor Top & Bottom Performers By Sector

S&P 500 Weekly Tear Sheet

Relative Performance Analysis

We noted that markets and sectors were overbought two weeks ago and could limit the upside. The “DeepSeek” announcement collided with that overbought condition, providing a pullback to the 50-DMA. Since then, the market ignored tariff and inflation news and moved sharply higher last week, testing all-time highs with Technology leading the charge. The market is now overbought, and even though bearish sentiment is rising, the flows into the market remain elevated. However, such is always the case just before a corrective action period. The market is well extended on many levels, and we are due for a pullback. However, given the current market momentum, such could take a few weeks to manifest. Rebalancing risk in portfolios will help navigate whatever comes next.

Technical Composite

The technical overbought/sold gauge comprises several price indicators (R.S.I., Williams %R, etc.), measured using “weekly” closing price data. Readings above “80” are considered overbought, and below “20” are oversold. The market peaks when those readings are 80 or above, suggesting prudent profit-taking and risk management. The best buying opportunities exist when those readings are 20 or below.

The current reading is 78.37 out of a possible 100.

Portfolio Positioning “Fear / Greed” Gauge

The “Fear/Greed” gauge is how individual and professional investors are “positioning” themselves in the market based on their equity exposure. From a contrarian position, the higher the allocation to equities, the more likely the market is closer to a correction than not. The gauge uses weekly closing data.

NOTE: The Fear/Greed Index measures risk from 0 to 100. It is a rarity that it reaches levels above 90.The current reading is 64.06 out of a possible 100.

Relative Sector Analysis

Most Oversold Sector Analysis

Sector Model Analysis & Risk Ranges

How To Read This Table

  • The table compares the relative performance of each sector and market to the S&P 500 index.
  • “MA XVER” (Moving Average Crossover) is determined by the short-term weekly moving average crossing positively or negatively with the long-term weekly moving average.
  • The risk range is a function of the month-end closing price and the “beta” of the sector or market. (Ranges reset on the 1st of each month)
  • The table shows the price deviation above and below the weekly moving averages.

We suggested two weeks ago that the very oversold condition in Technology was ripe for a rotation. That occurred, with stocks like NVDA and META leading the move higher. However, now that sector, along with international, emerging markets, gold, and gold miners, is well outside its normal risk range. Those markets should be rebalanced by taking profits and reducing exposure to target weights. There is decently elevated risk we will see a rotation out of these areas sooner than later.

Weekly SimpleVisor Stock Screens

We provide three stock screens each week from SimpleVisor.

This week, we are searching for the Top 20:

  • Relative Strength Stocks
  • Momentum Stocks
  • Fundamental & Technical Strength W/ Dividends

(Click Images To Enlarge)

RSI Screen

Momentum Screen

Technically Strong With Buy Rating

SimpleVisor Portfolio Changes

We post all of our portfolio changes as they occur at SimpleVisor:

Feb 11th

“As we discussed last week, the market has started to show signs of technical weakness in some of our longer-term indicators. As such, we have started to reduce exposure in very small amounts. As the market progresses, we will continue to take small actions to rebalance allocations and mitigate risk as needed.

“Today, we start with a slight rebalance of positions trading well above their target weights. We are reducing Costco (COST), Walmart (WMT), and Abbvie (ABBV) back to their allocated target weights and keeping the proceeds from those reductions in cash for the time being.”

Equity Model

  • Rebalance the following positions to their model portfolio weights
    • Costco (COST) – 4.5% of the portfolio
    • Walmart (WMT) – 1.75% of the portfolio

data.title: IXJ: Healthcare Sector Dashboard For February
data.description:
This monthly article series reports industry metrics in the healthcare sector. It is also a top-down analysis of healthcare ETFs like the Health Care Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLV), whose holdings have been used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italic describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each industry: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross Margin ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales, and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non-available when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set in a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for healthcare providers in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in this industry.

The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.

data.title: KCE: An Attractive Investment Option For Lofty Returns As Capital Markets Poised To Mark New Records
data.description:
SPDR® S&P Capital Markets ETF (NYSEARCA:KCE) appears to be a worth considering investment option for investors seeking to generate lofty returns both in the short and long-term. The ETF’s portfolio exposure to fundamentally strong companies in key financial industries, such as asset management & custody banks, investment banking & brokerage and financial exchanges & data, increases its potential to generate a solid price appreciation and dividend return. In addition, these three industries are poised to benefit from improved trading, IPO, venture, M&A and investing activities. Its holdings' average annual earnings growth estimate of 15% over the next 3 to 5 years appears strong enough to fuel price and cash returns. Therefore, I initiate coverage of KCE with a buy rating.

Capital Markets Outlook Signals Robust Growth

KCE’s capital markets portfolio is spread across three industries: asset management & custody banks, investment banking & brokerage and financial exchanges & data. The recent performance and outlook of companies in these industries demonstrate strong prospects for an uptrend in 2025. KCE’s top 5 holdings: Robinhood Markets (HOOD), Interactive Brokers (IBKR), LPL Financial Holdings (LPLA), The Goldman Sachs (GS), and Morgan Stanley (MS) - belongs to investment banking & brokerage industry. All these companies generated robust growth in the fourth quarter, with expectations for record-breaking numbers in 2025.

In particular, trading platforms are expected to witness high growth as rising stock markets, lower rates, and economic growth increase investors' confidence. In addition, trading platforms strategy of expanding their global foot-prints by permitting clients across the world to trade and conduct transactions contributed to the growth trend. This trend is expected to continue because trading platforms aggressively seek to capture more international clients by launching new products and offering a user-friendly platform.

For example, Robinhood Markets, the online trading and investment platform, saw a share price growth of more than 400% in the last twelve months amid its blistering growth rates. The company reported 115% year-over-year revenue growth in the December quarter, while its net income surged 10X year over year to a record $916 million. The revenue growth was driven by a substantial increase in trading volumes, subscribers, and deposits. Vlad Tenev, CEO and Co-Founder of Robinhood, believes that the company has a significant growth potential in 2025 because of its strategy of enabling anyone across the world to trade and make financial transactions.

Similarly, Interactive Brokers share price soared 127% in the past twelve months due to the robust financial and operational growth. Its fourth quarter revenue was $1.39 billion, compared with $1.14 billion in the same period last year. The adjusted earnings per share of $2.03 jumped from $1.52 in the year ago period. In Q4, the number of its customer accounts increased 30% year over year to 3.34 million, thanks to the robust international clients growth. Moreover, the company expects its revenue and earnings to increase at healthy pace in 2025, given a gigantic increase in its customer accounts and new product launches.

Whereas, with diversified business models, Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley, delivered solid share price and financial growth. Morgan Stanley’s net revenue of $16.2 billion grew from $12.9 billion in the year-ago period. The company saw growth across its businesses. Its institutional securities revenue increased 47% year over year, while the wealth management and investment management revenue increased at a double-double rate. Wall Street expects Morgan Stanley’s revenue and earnings to increase at a high single-digit rate in 2025 while Goldman’s earnings are expected to grow at a double-digit rate.

Whereas stocks from asset management & custody banks in KCE portfolio, such as Northern Trust Corporation (NTRS), The Bank of New York Mellon (BK), Blue Owl Capital (OWL), Ares Management Corporation (ARES) and Victory Capital Holdings (VCTR), are flourishing in easing monetary condition and steady economic growth. Shares of The Bank of New York Mellon rallied 60% in the past twelve-month on the back of record revenue and earnings. In 2024, the bank delivered record revenue of 18.4 billion and its earnings per share increased 18% year over year. The bank expects to extend its growth momentum, while Wall Street estimates 14% earnings growth on a mid-single-digit revenue increase.

Ares Capital Management and Victory Capital Holdings continued to generate solid returns for investors due to impressive financial and operational growth. In 2024, Ares Capital saw a record fundraising of $92.7 billion and its fee-paying AUM grew of 12% year-over-year. The company's management anticipates that improving transactions and significant pent-up demand is likely to drive its financial growth. Victory Capital Holdings share price doubled in the past twelve months on record financial metrics, with expectations for further growth in 2025 due to Amundi acquisition and ETF expansion.

Companies in the financial exchanges & data industry, such as Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), S&P Global (SPGI), Moody's Corporation (MCO), Nasdaq (NDAQ) and Morningstar (MORN) continued to generate a steady growth. After significantly surpassing adjusted earnings per share growth expectations in 2024, the S&P Global expects its adjusted revenue growth of 5%-7% in 2025 and adjusted diluted EPS to hover around $17.00 and $17.25 compared $15.70 per share in 2024.

Overall, it appears that capital markets are poised to accelerate the growth trend in 2025 because of improved market fundamentals. Therefore, investing in capital markets using investment options like SPDR® S&P Capital Markets ETF could be a great strategy.

KCE Characteristics Support a Buy Rating

SPDR® S&P Capital Markets ETF generated an exceptional total return of 84% since 2023, outperforming the S&P 500’s return of 57%. Its robust share price momentum is supported by its diversified and well-balanced portfolio of 63 fundamentally strong companies. While the portfolio offers diversification to three important industries in the financial sector, which we already discussed above, a well-balanced weight in each stock increases its potential to capitalize on the broader trends and lower risk factor. KCE’s top 10 holdings account for 19.5% of its entire portfolio, with a top holding making up 2.5%.

The fund's strategy of weighting stocks based on their fundamentals instead of a market-cap contributes to its share price momentum. For instance, Robinhood, the top-performing financial stock with strong fundamentals, is its largest holding. In addition, the majority of its top holdings belong to investment banking & brokerage industry, which is experiencing record-breaking financial and operational growth. Stocks from the asset management & custody banks and financial exchanges & data industries account for 43% and 22% of its entire portfolio.

According to the fund’s data, its portfolio has an average annual estimated earnings growth rate of 15% over the next 3 to 5 years. I believe double-digit earnings growth is sufficient to drive the share price upside momentum and cash returns. The ETF currently offers a dividend yield of 1.52%. The fund lifted its dividend by 16% in the trailing twelve months.

In addition, KCE's healthy earnings growth rate continues to balance its price to earnings ratio despite a solid share price growth in 2024. Its stock is currently trading around 17x forward PE. Its expense ratio of 0.40% is down from the median of all ETFs and in-line with most of its peers. With $555 million in assets under management and 61K in average shares trading volume, KCE’s liquidity factor is also strong enough to make it a worth considering option for the long and short-term investing. The ETF earned a buy rating with a high score of 4.40 based on the Seeking Alpha quant analysis. KCE is ranked at a third spot in its sub-asset class.

Risk Factors

The financial sector carries one of the highest volatility factors because of its correlation to external factors like monetary and economic conditions. A tighter than expected monetary policy and lower than expected economic growth can negatively influence the ETF’s performance. Its beta of 1.33 and high standard deviation of 18 also signals that the ETF can fall faster than the broader market index in downtrends.

In Conclusion

With the robust recent performance and solid outlook, investing in the capital markets could be a great strategy. In addition, investment vehicles like SPDR® S&P Capital Markets ETF offer a targeted exposure to fundamentally strong stocks in the capital markets. Its well-balanced portfolio of 63 stocks positions it to fully capitalize on the growth potential while lowering the risk factor. The ETF's cheap valuation, healthy dividend yield and low expense ratio also makes it a worth considering investment option.

data.title: S&P 500 Snapshot: Inches Away From Record High
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The S&P 500 ended its two-week skid, finishing up 1.5% from last Friday. The index is now just inches below its record close from January 23rd, 2025 and is up 4.19% year-to-date.

The table below summarizes the number of record highs reached each year dating back to 2013.

Here is a snapshot of the index over the past 5 days:

Here is a snapshot of the index from the past six months with a 50-day moving average:

As of February 14th, the U.S. Treasury put the closing yield on the 10-year note at 4.47% and the 2-year note at 4.26%. See our latest Treasury Snapshot here. ETFs associated with Treasuries include: iShares 1-3 Year Treasury Bond ETF (SHY), iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF (IEF), and iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT).

S&P 500: A Perspective on Drawdowns

On October 9, 2007, the S&P 500 reached a then all-time high, closing the day at 1565.15. Then on March 9, 2009, the index dropped ~57% off of its high from exactly 17 months before, closing the day at 676.53. This time period became known as the Global Financial Crisis. It took over 5 years before the index reached a new then all-time high on March 28, 2013, where it closed out at 1569.19. The chart below is a snapshot of record highs and selloffs since the 2007 peak reached on October 9, 2007.

What happens if we take out the Global Financial Crisis? Here's a snapshot of the same chart above where the start date has been changed to the trough reached on March 9, 2009. Note the recent selloffs in 2022.

Here are a few tables with the number of days of a 1% or greater change in either direction and the number of days of corrections (down 10% or more from the record high).

Here is a linear chart of the index since October 9, 2007:

Here is a linearly scaled version of the same chart with the 50- and 200-day moving averages. The index has been above its 200-day moving average since November 1st, 2023. For the past few weeks, the index and its 50-day moving average have been crossing each other, with the index currently ahead.

S&P 500: A Perspective on Volatility

For a sense of the correlation between the closing price and intraday volatility, the chart below overlays the S&P 500 since 2007 with the intraday price range. I've also included a 20-day moving average to identify trends in volatility. On December 18, the index experienced its largest intraday price volatility (3.45%) since November 30, 2022 (3.59%).

S&P 500 versus S&P Equal Weight

The S&P 500 is a market cap-weighted index which includes roughly the 500 largest U.S. stocks spanning 11 sectors. The S&P 500 Equal Weight Index includes the same constituents as the S&P 500, but each company is equally weighted at a fixed weight. So how do these two indexes match up against each other this year?

The S&P 500 is currently up 4.19% year-to-date, while the S&P Equal Weight is up 3.52% year-to-date.

ETFs associated with the S&P 500 include: iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPLG), and Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight ETF (RSP).
